title: 2026/03/25 - EW [Part 3]
displayTitle: 2026-03-25 - Education and Workforce Committee - Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill [Part 3]
topic: Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill
description: Wednesday 25 March 2026 | Education and Workforce Committee Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill Civil Contractors New Zealand - Fraser May, Communications and Advocacy Manager - Hugh Goddard Health Sector Committee of the Public Service Association; PSA DOC National Delegates Committee; PSA Te Pukenga Here Tikanga Mahi - Duane Leo, National Secretary - Tracy Klenner, Health and Safety Lead - Paula Warren, Delegate Tertiary Education Union - Megan Morris, Education and Industrial Campaigns Officer - Zoe Port, TEU member Retail NZ - Carolyn Young, Chief Executive - Denise Garland, Advocacy, Advice & Communications Manager - Yevgenya Chityan, Business & HR Advisor Bathurst Resources Limited - Richard Tacon, Chief Executive Officer - Fiona Bartier, General Manager Health Safety and Environment New Zealand Institute of Directors - Guy Beatson, GM of the Governance Leadership Centre - Herman Visagie, Incoming GM of the Governance Leadership Centre Health and Safety Association New Zealand - Mike O'Brien, HASANZ Independent Chair - Joe Bain, Incoming Executive Director and Current Member of the HASANZ Governance Group representing Engineering NZ - Joel Majer, Member of the HASANZ Governance Group representing Institute of Organisational Psychology Mike O'Brien Simpson Grierson - John Rooney, Partner - Mike Mercer, Senior Associate Safer Farms - Lindy Nelson, Sheep and Beef Farmer, Farm Without Harm Ambassador, Safer Farms Board Member and past Chair of Safer Farms Board - Natalie Bowie, Acting CEO E tu - Karena Brown, Lead Strategic Researcher Parallaxx Limited - Dave Tilton, Director Community Law Centres Aotearoa - Rupert O'Brien, Law Reform Coordinator The Outdoor Access Commission, Herenga a Nuku Aotearoa - Geoff Holgate, Regional Field Advisor
descriptionSummary: Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill. Civil Contractors New Zealand. Fraser May, Communications and Advocacy Manager

chunks.text: Hello, good morning, is it still? Yes. Yes. Wonderful. Just. We've been here a while. Welcome. In the interest of time, while everyone's getting themselves seated, we're in public session for the bill on the Health and Safety at Work Act. Can we just ask that you have 10 minutes, and that includes time for questions, and take the report or your submission as read, and highlight what you like, and the time is over to you.

Understood. I'm ready to go. Yes, please. Great. Okay. Good morning. Thank you, Madam Chair. Thank members of the committee for the opportunity to appear for Civil Contractors New Zealand regarding Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill. I'm Fraser May. I'm the communications and advocacy manager at CCNZ. We're the National Association for Horizontal Construction.

And with me here today, I've got Managing Director of Pipeline and Civil, Mr. Hugh Goddard. Hi. He's a CCNZ member. His business is primarily involved in construction and maintenance of water networks, but he's also a board member of Construction Health and Safety New Zealand and an independent director of roading company Informax. So yeah, pretty good board experience. CCNZ, we've got more than 800 member businesses and organizations responsible for constructing and maintaining roads, water, energy, and more.

Our sector operates in high-risk environments, underground tunnels, trenches, large machinery, live traffic lanes, soil stabilization halfway up a cliff. You name it, the members are out there doing it. Our comments are pretty simple, really. Reform should deliver genuine safety improvements, provide clarity for duty holders, and be practical and workable across infrastructure delivery at all scales.

We will have the submission as read. I just wanted to focus on a couple of key points. The cost of industry-- injuries, sorry, to business is very real and has been projected in the billions annually. But that's to the economy as well, and most of all to the workers who experience the injuries. So I think, we've all got a stake in stopping that from happening. We support the bill's objectives of clarifying and streamlining health and safety obligations.

We do welcome emphasis on prioritizing critical risk, but we feel that the proposals that have been put forward do not adequately capture the risk, and we feel some changes are needed. So risks, we do not feel, are dependent on business size. So, the 20 work definition of a small PCBU is not workable. We feel it's arbitrary, and it's not going to really be applicable to construction settings.

Those businesses I talk to working halfway up a cliff, they are often 10 workers maybe. But they operate in some of the highest risk settings out there, and they need to manage that. We appreciate clear guidance on governance responsibilities, but we think a bit more is needed, especially where an officer holds a governance and an operational role within a business.

We need clarity on that. We need fair regulatory responsibilities, and there needs to be strong accountability for upstream clients. We can't put people at risk of injury to cut corners or cut costs. Clients pay for the project, and they set the table for success or failure. So having them say, "We don't play the health and safety game with me," could put people at risk, and we can't have that happening.

Everyone's got a role to play. What we're asking for is robust, industry-connected regulatory guidance and active consultation with our member businesses to ensure the changes can be implemented effectively and supported by practical codes of practice. We'll leave time for questions, but I'm going to hand over to Hugh now for his perspective and a couple of examples from the contractors' perspective.

Yeah. Thanks very much, Fraser. And hi, everyone. So I guess, giving an example around the proposed PCBU carve-out. So in our sector, risk doesn't change depending on whether the person standing beside the trench or a live service or a piece of plant is employed by a business with 15 workers or with 50. Risk is the same.

So the consequences if things go wrong are the same, so I think that's a key thing. On a typical civil project, we may have a main contractor, specialist subcontractors, labor-only teams, surveyors, traffic management, plant operators, reinstatement teams, all working together. And sometimes this equates to hundreds of people from different companies on-site at any one time. So I guess the challenge is for a WorkSafe person going into that site, how do they know if they're dealing with a PCBU that actually has less than or greater than 20 workers?

And if the law applies differently depending on the size, it doesn't simplify site management. In practice, the head contractor controlling the PCBU will still need one common standard across the site. Mm-hmm. So that's just going to create, instead of less bureaucracy, it's going to create more administration, more headaches, I guess, and more challenges and confusion.

I think we've covered off that small businesses doesn't equate to smaller risks. So I think that point was well made by Fraser. And in critical risk, we certainly support direction in principle, and I think the more important issue from a contractor's perspective is defining and making sure the critical controls are identified, understood, resourced, monitored. That's where the rubber hits the road in terms of controls rather than just identifying the risks, and that's what prevents catastrophic events from occurring.

Obviously, in New Zealand, we don't have the kind of health and safety record that justifies lowering the bar, and we should be looking to raise standards, not dilute them. The cost to harm is significant. It's borne by the sector itself through disruption, reduced productivity, lost capability, and poor outcomes for workers and families, but it's also borne more widely through ACC and the public health system.

Mm-hmm. So, I think there's a big challenge here. And in my view, the pathway to a safer, more productive construction sector is not to water down safety laws, to have a strong and well resourced regulator, clear and practical guidance around what good looks like, and an industry where expectations are consistent and understood.And that will help create a future where work is done by people who are safe, healthy, well-trained, properly qualified, more engaged, and ultimately, more productive.

So the practical message really is the biggest opportunity here isn't to increase complexity by creating a two-tier system of duties. Let's create a system that is clear, more practical, and more useful for the people actually delivering work on the ground. So, our original submission to the MB consultation and subsequent discussions with the minister call for clear guidance through ACOPS- Mm... and similar tools, and that remains unchanged. And thanks very much.

And looking forward to questions. Thank you. Just a couple of quick questions from myself. We've heard a lot about the carve-out for small business, but we have, and not to just throw it out, we have heard from some that say that that is incredibly helpful, like in farming, for example. Do you see a workable way where you could carve out industry rather than size?

So, for example, we've heard from transport and from yourselves that you don't see a differentiation between small and large for risk. But yet there might be small offices, like an accounting firm, where that small PCBU works. Mm. So do you see that working from your perspective? I might set off on that one if I could here.

Yeah. I think it does depend on the level of risk. So absolutely, that might make sense for a dairy owner, to have less paperwork or less unclear obligations. But what risks are they facing, is the critical thing here. So, if we're working in the top 10% of risk the whole time, and pretty much every civil construction site out there will be- Mm... we need to manage that well. Whether that's, and how that's handled through the legislation matters.

Mm. I think, a blanket cutoff will be very difficult for us to manage because it creates a double standard for people that are working, and it's not really applicable. Yeah. I'd also just add, I guess, around what we call risk amplifiers, so things like fatigue, psychosocial harm- Mm... stress, all these sorts of factors are risk amplifiers.

So although you might be working around an excavator- Mm... that could kill you if something went wrong. If that person's tired, subject to bullying, harassment, or whatever- Mm or fatigue or other sort of factors, that's going to obviously influence the outcome. And I think, having less focus on those risks and obviously musculoskeletal is a big area as well.

There's a significant challenge in the construction sector. And by de-emphasizing the importance of businesses to manage those, you're creating some challenges. Yeah. Phil. Just, I want to make sure I understand your main point, and thank you for a very compelling submission. But you seem to be, your argument, I think, if I hear you right, is that the level of risk is unrelated to the size- Yeah... of the enterprise or the number of employees. Is that right?

Correct. Correct. So the PCBU cutoff, the two-tier system that this bill creates- Mm doesn't pertain to actual risk. Is that right? Correct. Yeah. So that's what I'm saying about having it carved out, industry rather than size. Yeah. I guess, in your submission you say the committee should ensure the bill does not create a legislative signal that these risks, that is the psychosocial risks outside the critical risk definition, that these risks are secondary or optional, but doesn't it, by definition, it's central to the concept of the whole

bill, that those risks are being deprioritized, aren't they? Well, I suppose you just take mental health as an example. I'm not sure if I fully, maybe you come back to your question- Yeah... to make sure I'm clear on it. But we're losing a person a week to suicide in construction.

Right. So as a result of lots of factors. Yep. But obviously by de-emphasizing that for smaller businesses, which actually the majority of, I know 90 plus percent of construction businesses in New Zealand are small businesses by definition, and probably fall- Yeah... largely within the criteria of less than 20. We're not addressing a massive issue that we've got and I think that's where the challenge is that we need to tackle.

Thank you. Question from Vanessa Wenick. So in your submission, you highlight catastrophic risk. Mm. Can I just have an example of what you might mean by catastrophic risk? Explosion of a bitumen tanker. Mm. It's a very low likelihood of something like that happening. But obviously the consequences are significant.

So by, I guess, how we capture and utilize the word likelihood- Okay... in this case, needs to be expanded- Yeah... in the bills further. Can I just-- So when you're doing a risk analysis matrix, basically- Yeah... the likelihood and the outcomes are the kind of thing. To me, the outcomes are the only things that have been focused on potentially in looking at this and that schedule of whether it's death or injury to eyes or those kind of things.

Whereas actually, and then the likelihood of that happening creates a critical risk rather than a catastrophic risk, which is probably never happen. But if it does- Mm... holy crap, you know, sort of thing. Yes. Yeah. That's it. Yeah. Yeah. If we lose a fuel line to the Interislander or something, that's like- Yeah. So just one thing I wanted to raise was one thing that we do support, and we probably didn't talk about this, and that is the role of WorkSafe and the prioritization.

Mm-hmm. I think the problem we've got at the moment is that it's not entirely clear what people's responsibilities are. Industry has guidance like bitumen burns cards or- Mm... bitumen safety handbooks. Mm. But getting WorkSafe to endorse those and say, "This is a reasonable standard- Yeah... this is reasonably practicable," is very, very difficult at the moment- Correct. Mm... to this stage. And that is what we really need.

Yeah. And I think- That's the role for them... the way that the legislation has looked at that is perhaps more spot on as long as WorkSafe's properly resourced and encouraged to do that job, that that would support that. Thank you. That is all the time we've got with you, but we found your submission very helpful and- Yeah. Thank you guys... thank you for your time. Thanks so much. Thank you very much, Mia.

Thank you. Wonderful. Yes. Perfect. We have now got Duane Leo, Tracey Klenner, and Paula Warren from the Health Sector Committee of the PSA. Welcome. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that includes time for questions, and the time is yours now. Over to you.

Tēnā koe chair and members of the committee. Thank you for the chance for us to speak today on behalf of our members and the union. My name is Duane Leo. I'm one of the national secretaries for the New Zealand Public Service Association, and to my left, I have Tracey Klenner, who is our health and safety lead, and I also have Paula Warren, who's our PSA National Delegates Committee and also one of our national delegates at DOC.

So you've received three submissions from different parts of the PSA. One overall submission, one from the members of our health and safety sector committee, and one from the members of the PSA National Delegates Committee at Department of Conservation. We have over 96,000 members working in all parts of public and community services around the country.

That's a wide range of experiences and perspectives. Our health and safety sector committee and the DOC National Delegates Committee wanted to be here to share their perspectives on the bill with you, and we're disappointed you don't have the opportunity to hear from them. So in terms of the proposal, we have summary point one. The PSA submits that the bill, as drafted, risks weakening worker protections by narrowly focusing on critical risks and carving out small PCBUs for core duties.

Mm. So in terms of that, the bill's emphasis is narrow, and the definition of critical risk could exclude high-frequency, everyday hazards, multiskeletal injuries, slips, trips, falls, workplace violence, and incidents that drive most ACC costs and harm to workers. Because of the poorly worded definition of critical risk, all PCBUs will no longer have to prioritize risks that fall outside the definition but can have quite significant health impacts.

A clear example of this would be where workers are exposed to a combination of chemicals or hazardous substances that together cause long-term health risks, for example, the likes of cancer. These types of exposures are not captured by Schedule 2, but are very common in instances with firefighters, lab workers, wood processing, manufacturing.

All PCBUs would no longer have to prioritize managing these risks, directly resulting in a less safe workplace. Using the ACC occupational disease schedule to define critical health risks is inappropriate and risks leaving important work-related illnesses outside the scope of duty. Thanks, Duane. Kia ora. I'm Tracey Klenner, as Duane mentioned.

Our concern more broadly is with the problem with the small PCBU carve-out. So, we believe this is exempting or weakening duties for small PCBUs employing 20 or under workers will create a two-tier health and safety system. Fragmented accountability in shared workplaces and likely increase injuries.

All workers have the right to go home from work safe. It shouldn't matter how small their workplace is. It'll be workers and their families and ACC that will bear the cost of these unmanaged risks. And as the union for people working in health and safety regulation, we also submit that in workplaces where PCBUs have to deal with both large and small organizations, this will create confusion and enforcement difficulties if different standards apply.

So, if we look at this from the point of view of my workplace, Department of Conservation, most of our staff are working with people who are from small businesses. So they're dealing with rural contractors, they're dealing with the people who build huts, who fix our cars, and they're going into other people's workplaces.

And what that means is that we will have to put a lot more cost and effort into managing the risks that are faced by our people working with those businesses. Mm. So there's not going to be an overall reduction in cost. There's just going to be a higher confusion. So when I have raised this issue with a health and safety manager, they have said, "Well, everybody who comes into our workplace will have to comply with our laws."

That means that we will have to re-look at every contract, including for concessionaires- Mm... who work on our lands. So, it's just going to be very difficult to manage. In addition, we have concerns about the approved codes of practice and the regulator role. Approved codes of practice must be developed with WorkSafe, with the participation of unions and workers, be regularly reviewed and act as guidance without setting a ceiling on reasonably practicable duties.

We're narrowing WorkSafe's oversight to only catastrophic risks, risks enforcement gaps, and inconsistent sector outcomes. We also have concerns about the problem with relying on other enactments, so proposed Section 35 treats compliance with other regulatory regimes as equivalent to Health and Safety at Work Act duties.

It's unsafe. Many laws, wildlife, marine, education, earthquake compliance, corrections regulations, are designed for other purposes and do not protect worker safety. This should not proceed. So in terms of the economic and system consequences, so if these changes are made and everyday hazards are deprioritized, ACC claims, health system demand, and productivity losses, they will rise, shifting costs on to the state, onto workers, and onto the workers' families and communities. We all want a country where we're all confident that our children and

our family members and our friends will come home safe from work. The bill makes this less likely, and we do not support it. In terms of our ask, what we're asking from the committee, we're asked to recommend that the bill does not progress in its current form. Please require amendments to retain universal health and safety duties for all PCBUs. Remove the small PCBU carve-out to keep all workers safe. Broaden the scope beyond the critical risks.Tighten Section 35 and mandate WorkSafe worker and union participation in the approved codes of

practice. And thank you, that's our presentation for this morning. Thank you very much. We have three questions. Hopefully, we can get through all of them. We have the first question from Carl. In the industries and the work sites that the PSA covers, is there any correlation between level of risk and size of enterprise, number of employees in a PCBU?

Sue. Yeah, I think there is some clear research, but really hard to come by, that there are a number of more risks in smaller places, and because larger places, as corrections, for example, where they do have some very high risks- Yeah... but they have really robust health and safety systems.

So there's higher risk- That includes workers-... in smaller workplaces. Because there is often less of an internal system in the background, right? Right. Yeah. Corrections have got a multitude of health and safety governance committees, and workers are involved right from the floor up. Similarly, at the Department of Conservation, another place with quite high level critical risks.

Yep. People working under helicopters and in helicopters. And yet this bill weakens the protections for those smaller workplaces. The most vulnerable. Yep. Yeah. Thank you. Yeah. And so again, generally outside the public service, the businesses that we work with that have the highest risks- Yep... helicopters, small boats, construction at height, abseiling for weed control, the use of toxic sprays, these are all small businesses- Mm... that we use for those.

Thank you. If we can try and get to Carl Bates and then Ricardo, and then Desmond. Thanks, Chair. Thanks for your submission. In your experience, in all of the involvement you've had in health and safety, one of the aims of this bill is to reduce the compliance burden for business, in the health and safety management of risk.

Where have your members or your own engagement, any recent examples of recommending changes to health and safety practice that have removed unnecessary compliance burdens in your workplaces, or is it always layering on another layer of requirements, activity, compliance?

I think most of the workplaces where our members were, it is the simple process of including the worker voice in any conversation that's held around health and safety. It doesn't create lots of extra time or complexity, and it works well, and conversations occur. Even in a small, like if we look at Ministry for Women, for example, it's a small place. They don't have a huge health and safety system.

They work alongside the government health and ... by the Public Service Commission. And, so it doesn't create lots of extra paperwork and lots of extra time because they share that among one another. So, does, is that answer to your question? So my question is- Yeah... are there any examples of where you've gone, "That process actually isn't needed. We're taking it away," that you can think of?

No, I don't think ... I think the issue here from our perspective is where there's good employers and when they're working together with their staff, with the unions, there's not seen to be that much detail and complexity. It's embedded in business as usual. They don't see it as an over and above and extra thing they have to do, and that's with good employers, and that's the rationale there.

And in our submission, we do note the surveys that we ran, both with our wider health and safety advocates, but also with members within, for example, WorkSafe. Where there's, again, the perception that there's that complexity just isn't there where you've got a good functioning health and safety workplace, and they're constantly looking to renew, refresh, and update. So I don't necessarily think you'd call that additional layers of bureaucracy. It's just they're constantly making sure that they're addressing the need in the workplace.

Thank you. Yeah. Thank you. That's all the time we have with you. We appreciate your- Thank you... very detailed submissions and your participation in the process. Lovely, Chair. Thank you, Committee. All right. We now have the Tertiary Education Union joining us online. And we have Megan Morris and Zoe Port.

Welcome. Zoe, have we got Megan separately, or is she going to be in the same room with you? No. Kia ora, sorry. Megan, I've been told, is also in the Zoom. She's in a different location. It might be that she's just changing. Here she is. Oh, here we are. Wonderful. Hi. Okay. We have 10 minutes with you, that includes time for questions. Over to you.

I'm starting, eh, Zoe? Tena koutou. Ko Megan Morris toku ingoa. Ko Te Pō Matauranga me Te Pō Ahumahi tohu tuaharange Aotearoa. I hold a national role as our union's education industrial campaigns officer, and my experience includes about 15 years as a health and safety trainer for the CTU WorkSafe Rep program, along with policy development and consultation.

I did a lot during COVID, the pandemic, and I've worked across health, in aged care in my previous life before I joined the tertiary sector, and I've been in the tertiary sector for about the last 20 years. More recently, though, we've been working increasingly in the, or I have, in the psychosocial hazard and risk area around developing courses, providing advice and technical support internally and externally with other unions.

As our submission states, we work across the entirety of the tertiary sector. We have over 11,000 members, and we strongly oppose the proposed changes to this billThe stated objectives of the proposed amendment bill are to reduce unnecessary compliance costs, increase certainty for business, and finally, to support continued reductions in the incidents of workplace fatalities, injuries, and illnesses.

And it says a lot to those of us who work predominantly with workers, that the priorities of the proposed amendments seem to be that compliance and businesses first, and worker health and safety is the last objective. One of the other stated areas of concerns around leading to the amendments was around the confusion and over-compliance from many PCBUs in the consultation period, which to be clear from a worker perspective and the unions, was overwhelmingly excluded worker voice through that process, all of the meetings held around the country.

So we don't genuinely feel that this is a good representation of any necessary amendments to the current legislation. The focus on critical risk is really concerning for us. It really exposes the gendered nature of the proposed changes, who ignore women who make up largely about approximately 50% of the workforce. And for all of those who work in female-dominated occupational areas like health and education.

The biggest issues that we deal with are in the sort of hazards and risk around in the psychosocial space. So things like workload, continuous restructuring in our sector is a massive problem, and that creates huge issues around stress and fatigue, et cetera. Also, the amendments largely ignore the musculoskeletal issues like slips, trips, and falls.

And again, harassment, bullying, violence in the workplace. The psychosocial area is often seen as invisible, and the impacts of being affected by those sorts of things is really wide-ranging and can often, will generally be taken home to family, friends, et cetera.

If we look outside of our sector, for example, the focus on critical risk for small businesses could potentially push the cost of PPE onto workers. For example, workers in the care and support sector who already been denied pay equity settlements, they travel massive distances, and they use their own vehicles with a completely inadequate petrol reimbursement. But they're one of the most vulnerable groups in our workforce.

And if you are asking them, well, the bill could potentially ask them to provide their own PPE, for example. So they already earn low wages, they cover their own petrol, they use their own cars, and now they're having to provide their own PPE because the type of work that they do would not fit the definition of critical risk that has been put into this bill.

Or if you look into sort of trades and manufacturing and spaces like that, most of the PPE available for workers is actually designed by men for men. So it doesn't fit women very well, and if you happen to be a petite woman, you've got serious problems trying to find PPE that actually fits. And the point of the PPE is that it is meant to provide appropriate protection, whether it's overalls or respirators or helmets, whatever it might be, steel cap boots, et cetera.

It's meant to provide you with that element of protection so that when you do your job, you can do it safely. That would not occur. The amendment bill will, in effect, create a two-tier system of managing health and safety, which in our opinion, will only increase confusion and uncertainty for PCBUs, not reduce it. It will also increase exposure for workers to hazards and risks, especially in the small business space, and do nothing to reduce the incidents of workplace fatalities, serious injuries, or illnesses. So,

if we think about the point of the exercise with health and safety legislation is about protecting workers and ensuring that they can do their job safely and well, and go home to their families safe and well, and not have ongoing impacts from the job that they do affecting their everyday lives.

I don't believe that the changes in this legislation will actually address that in any way, shape, or form. I would also note that worker voice is absolutely critical to ensuring good compliance with legislation. So in our sector, most of the employers are relatively large, and they do have reasonable systems.

But the issues of working with workers and their health and safety reps across the sector is good in some places and not so good in others. But we work really hard with those employers to ensure that they do have robust systems in place that can protect our workers.

But it's not always perfect. And we've got in the psychosocial space, in particular, those are really hard to deal with. And it is critical for us that we ensure that there are systems in place that can make that an easier process to manage.

And again, don't believe that the changes in this bill will do that. I might just stop there and allow Zoe Thanks so much, Megan. On that, I would like to just add, so Kia Ora, Dr Zoe Port, lecturer in management at Massey University and TU member. And my research expertise is particularly in the area of managing psychosocial risks and creating healthy work. I teach and research in this area.

And I guess one thing I wanted to say is our sector, the tertiary sector, it is meant to be grounded in evidence. We must be evidence-informed in our teaching. We don't just convey whatever ideas we personally like to. We base it on the research and the evidence, and I'd posit that many of the claims upon which the most egregious changes in this bill are proposed, that Megan has mentioned, they are simply not grounded in evidence, and in many cases they contradict evidence and expert opinion, including experts from the

past Pike River Royal Commission. And just one point I want to drive home around psychosocial risk and hazards as well, and why it is problematic that it essentially gets overlooked by the narrow definition of critical risk, is that psychosocial hazards are unique. They arise from the way work is designed, organized, and managed, things like excessive workload, like Megan has said.

And the harm from them doesn't just stop when the worker leaves the office at the end of the day or leaves the workplace. Unlike a physical hazard that can only get me at work, psychosocial harm, it can continue to manifest in the worker's mind, and the impact can continue to weigh on them. And it manifests not only in terms of mental health outcomes, but also physical.

For example, there's a huge body of international evidence on, say, psychosocial hazards and cardiovascular disease. So just to drive that home, we cannot be overlooking psychosocial hazards as this definition does, because they cause just as much harm as more visible hazards. I think being mindful of time, I will leave that there. Thank you. We have run out of time with you, but do appreciate your participation in the process. Thank you for being with us today.

Kia ora. Kia ora. We'll move on now to Retail New Zealand, who are in the room, and we'll come back to Bathurst Resources if they join us. Thank you very much. We've got Carolyn Young, Denise Garland, and Yevgenia. Yevgenia. Thank you. Chair.

Time is yours. Sorry, 10 minutes, includes time for questions. Sorry. Thank you- That's okay... very much for the time. Really appreciate the opportunity to be here. My name's Carolyn, as you mentioned, my colleague, Denise, and Yev. So look, we know that you've read the submission, so we're not going to go through that. Just want to highlight a couple of things, then we open for questions.

Look, in principle, we support the bill. We think there's a couple of areas that need further clarification. So our concerns are that this bill would potentially give reason to believe that small retailers may not be liable to manage their risks adequately. So, prioritizing critical risks means we don't want them to ignore hazards, which is not the intention of the bill.

We feel that critical risks need to have some greater clarity. And we don't want a small retailer or a small business, of which in New Zealand there are many, that's what we're made up of, that's our sort of fabric, we don't want them to have the mentality that it doesn't apply to them. So, we think that you have to manage small risks because a lot of small risks can end up being a larger risk. So we need to be aware that language and education is going to be really important to make sure that business owners really understand where the threshold sits and

where it crosses over for their business and their environment. One of the things, to give you an example, when some of the initial wording of the bill came out and there was some announcements around it was going to make it easier for SMEs to navigate health and safety, we had a member call us, and I spoke to them directly myself, and they sort of said, "Okay, well, we won't need to worry about all of these things.

Can I now not have to pay for... We've got a health and safety consultant that we use that manages all of our things. We've got these big manuals. We won't have to worry about that." And I'm like, "Taiho, little bit premature. We're not there yet. That was months and months ago, and they were ready to change then." So just remembering that headlines that come out aren't the detail, and SMEs don't have, we've been talking quite extensively in the office, that SMEs don't have the time or skills to navigate and find their ways

through legislation and the detail. And what we've seen is that, for example, the definition of critical risks are included in Schedule 1 through ACC, talking about the death and loss of limb, et cetera. But the detail of what could cause that critical risk is not really present.

So you really have to evaluate what the various sources are to understand how to define a risk. So if it's about an object falling from a height, it's included in Schedule 1A, and then you have to go to the regs, which says it has to be managed in accordance with regs 5 to 8. And then you have to understand those regs.

And for a small business to be able to navigate all of that, I think is quite complicated.
